Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I had to work around a lot of things outside of school. I tried to study in blocks when I could, usually late at night or early morning when my living situation was quieter. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often pace around my room while reviewing notes or act out concepts physically to remember them. With only about 14 hours a week to dedicate to studying, I had to be strategic, but my attendance suffered because life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then I'd have intense study sessions right before something was due. I wished I could be more consistent, but between work and other responsibilities with limited resources, regularity was a luxury I couldn't always afford. Deadlines were really what drove my schedule more than any personal routine.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, honestly. I'd get this wave of anxiety thinking about what I might be missing or what deadline was creeping up. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if I'd missed something important. The scared feeling in my chest was often what pushed me to open the LMS, even when I didn't feel mentally ready to engage with the content.
Q4: There were periods where logging in became almost automatic, especially when I was deep into watching video lectures. I'd watch over 250 minutes of video content because that format worked well for me—I could pause, rewind, and physically practice concepts. Once I got into a rhythm of checking discussions and watching videos, it felt more natural. But that routine would break whenever life threw something difficult at me.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me. Being a kinesthetic learner, I could watch demonstrations and then physically try things myself. I also found the discussion boards helpful because seeing how others understood concepts gave me different perspectives. I posted about 12 times in discussions because engaging with peers, even just a little, helped solidify ideas in my mind.
Q6: I often delayed or minimized my use of reading-heavy materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited internet access sometimes and low resources, downloading everything wasn't always practical. I also ignored some optional quizzes because my anxiety made me avoid anything that felt like a test when I wasn't prepared. If something required too much passive reading, I'd skim it at best.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. Given that I scored really well on assignments—like 98.89%—I prioritized anything that helped me produce quality work. If I could physically engage with the material or if it would clearly help my grade, I was all in. Abstract or theoretical stuff without clear application felt like a waste of my limited study hours.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap for my anxiety, honestly. Required ones I had to do regardless of how scared I felt, so I'd push through. But optional ones? My brain would spiral into 'what if I do it wrong' or 'what if it's a waste of time I don't have.' The pressure of required work actually helped me focus, while optional stuff felt like it could expose my weaknesses without any safety net.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also made physical flashcards and would literally walk around my room acting out processes or scenarios to memorize them. My notes were extensive, and I'd practice problems by hand rather than through the LMS interface.
Q10: Most of my real learning happened away from the screen. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, but then I'd spend hours afterward physically practicing concepts, drawing diagrams, or explaining things out loud to myself. My kinesthetic approach meant I needed to move and do things with my hands. The LMS would just show one video view, but that video might fuel three hours of active, physical studying.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture or reading once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, and I couldn't afford to waste time with buffering or connection issues. So I became really good at grabbing materials in one session and then studying them independently. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the hours of review afterward.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt almost impossible. My anxiety would spike so high that even opening the LMS made my heart race. When stress from life outside school piled up—money issues, family stuff—the course felt like too much. My attendance dropped to under 50% partly because some days I was just too scared and overwhelmed to show up, physically or virtually.
Q13: All of those things affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant because I'd study late at night after dealing with other responsibilities. Confusion would sometimes paralyze me—I'd stare at the LMS not knowing where to start. Competing responsibilities meant the course often got pushed aside until panic set in. My task completion was only 33% because I simply couldn't keep up with everything while managing real-life demands.
Q14: Many times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be too anxious to actually absorb anything, just going through motions to feel like I was doing something. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't retained a single thing because I was worried about a bill or a family situation. The access was there, but meaningful engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when my anxiety was high. If an activity felt overwhelming or if I wasn't confident I could do it well, I'd avoid it even knowing it was important. It's like my brain was protecting me from potential failure, but it made things worse. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt less scared, but that feeling rarely came, so things piled up.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They terrified me, but they also forced me to act. I'd often have bursts of intense LMS activity right before something was due—watching videos, checking discussion posts, downloading materials. Without deadlines, I might have drifted entirely. They created structure when my own life couldn't provide it, even if they also spiked my stress levels dramatically.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I got positive feedback on assignments, it gave me a boost of confidence that helped me engage more. Knowing I could score well—like my 98.89% on assignments—motivated me to keep trying despite everything. But waiting for feedback was agonizing; my anxiety would spiral imagining the worst. Once I knew I was doing okay, I could breathe and re-engage.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize when I could engage. But the structure also felt rigid when life got chaotic—I couldn't always follow the intended pace. The video-heavy content worked great for my learning style. However, when I fell behind, the structure made it feel impossible to catch up without drowning in anxiety.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn kinesthetically by pausing and practicing. Discussion boards were manageable too. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks or readings—my completion rate suffered because I couldn't keep track of everything with my limited time and resources. Clearer prioritization of what was truly essential versus supplementary would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I had high engagement—logging in frequently, watching lots of videos, posting in discussions—but my assignment completion was really low at 4.1%. For me, it was about capacity and fear. I was engaging with learning, but actually submitting work felt like a final judgment that my anxiety made terrifying. Also, life circumstances meant I couldn't always finish what I started.
Q21: That resonates with me too, in a way. My exam score was 84.39% despite my low attendance and task completion. I studied intensively offline, and when I did engage with the LMS, I made it count. Quality over quantity, I guess. Someone looking at my low activity might assume I wasn't learning, but I was—just not in ways the system could track. Resilience meant finding ways to succeed despite obstacles.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They capture patterns of activity timing pretty well. But they completely miss the depth of engagement—whether someone was truly learning or just clicking through. They miss offline study, emotional state, and life context. My logs would show sporadic access, but they'd never show the hours of physical practice and memorization I did away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just showing up. It's when concepts stick with me and I find myself thinking about them even outside of study time. For me, engagement is physical—pacing, practicing, doing. It's also emotional; when I'm engaged, I feel both scared of failing and motivated to understand. True engagement is internal and can't always be measured by clicks or logins.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much fear I pushed through to engage at all. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my anxiety before studying, or the physical ways I learned by moving around my room. It wouldn't show that behind every login was a battle with my own mind, or that my low resources meant every hour of study required sacrificing something else. My resilience is invisible to the data.
